diagrams.title: Traditional Organization vs Decentralized Organization Structure
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing hierarchical vs distributed authority structures
elements: Traditional Org: CEO → Managers → Employees (vertical hierarchy)
Decentralized Org: Distributed Network with peer-to-peer connections
Central node in traditional vs distributed nodes in decentralized
Centralized decision bottleneck vs collective decision making
Single point of failure vs distributed resilience
Clear chain of command vs consensus-based coordination
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental structural differences between centralized and decentralized organizations, including authority distribution, decision-making flow, and failure points

diagrams.title: Decentralized Organization Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the layered architecture of a decentralized organization
elements: Layer 1: User Interface (Web/Mobile Apps)
Layer 2: Governance Layer (Voting, Proposals, Rules)
Layer 3: Smart Contract Layer (Automated Execution)
Layer 4: Blockchain Layer (Immutable Ledger)
Layer 5: Token Economy Layer (Incentives, Rewards, Staking)
External Data: Oracles for real-world data input
Communication: P2P Network connections
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the multi-layered architecture of DOs and how each layer contributes to decentralization, automation, and trustless operation

diagrams.title: Decision Making Process in a Decentralized Organization
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing how decisions are proposed, voted on, and executed in a DO
elements: Step 1: Member proposes an idea/action
Step 2: Proposal submitted to governance smart contract
Step 3: Token-weighted voting period begins
Step 4: Vote tallying (Yes/No/Abstain)
Step 5: Threshold check (e.g., 50%+1 or supermajority)
Step 6: If passed → Execute via smart contract
Step 7: If failed → Proposal rejected
Step 8: Results recorded on blockchain
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete lifecycle of decentralized decision-making including proposal submission, voting mechanisms, and automated execution

diagrams.title: Governance Mechanisms in Decentralized Organizations
diagrams.description: Classification diagram showing different governance models used in DOs
elements: Token-based Governance: 1 token = 1 vote
Reputation-based Governance: Contribution history determines weight
Liquid Democracy: Delegated voting rights
Quadratic Voting: Square root of tokens calculates voting power
Conviction Voting: Time-weighted voting with gradual conviction
diagrams.purpose: Students learn various governance mechanisms and understand how different voting systems impact fairness, manipulation resistance, and participant engagement

charts.title: Traditional Organization vs Decentralized Organization vs DAO
charts.content: Traditional Org: Centralized authority, Hierarchical structure, Manual processes, Single point of failure, Paper/email based communication, Centralized database, Profit-driven, Limited transparency. Decentralized Organization: Distributed authority, Network structure, Smart contract automation, Distributed failure points, Blockchain-based communication, Distributed ledger, Multi-stakeholder, High transparency. DAO: Token-based governance, Code-is-law principle, Fully autonomous, Immutable rules, Cryptographic security, Community-owned, Collective decision-making, Programmable incentives

charts.title: Lifecycle of a Proposal in a DO
charts.content: Start → Submit Proposal → Deposit tokens (stake) → Discussion period → Voting period → Count votes → Check quorum → If passed: Execute → Record on blockchain → Distribute rewards → If failed: Return stake → End

charts.title: Types of Decentralized Organizations
charts.content: Decentralized Organizations (DO): Organizations using blockchain for transparency and coordination. Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAO): Fully autonomous, code-driven organizations. Decentralized Autonomous Corporations (DAC): Commercial DAs with profit generation. Decentralized Autonomous Societies (DAS): Social structures organized through DAOs. Decentralized Applications (dApps): Applications running on distributed networks with tokenized governance.

charts.title: Key Characteristics Comparison: DO vs DAO vs DAC vs DAS
charts.content: DO: Purpose - Distributed decision-making, Governance - Mixed (on/off-chain), Automation - Partial, Legal status - Often unclear. DAO: Purpose - Fully automated governance, Governance - On-chain voting, Automation - Full, Legal status - Experimental. DAC: Purpose - Commercial services, Governance - Token holder democracy, Automation - High, Legal status - Emerging. DAS: Purpose - Social coordination, Governance - Community-based, Automation - Variable, Legal status - Conceptual.

branches: Definition & Principles: Distributed authority, peer-to-peer coordination, transparency, immutability
Architecture: Multi-layer design (UI, Governance, Smart Contracts, Blockchain, Token Economy)
Governance Models: Token-based, Reputation-based, Liquid Democracy, Quadratic Voting, Conviction Voting
Types: DO, DAO, DAC, DAS, dApps
Key Features: Smart contracts, Token incentives, Consensus mechanisms, Voting systems
Decision Making: Proposal → Voting → Execution → Recording
Benefits: Transparency, Resilience, Global participation, Reduced corruption
Challenges: Scalability, Legal recognition, User adoption, Smart contract risks
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview of all aspects of decentralized organizations including their structure, governance, types, and real-world implications for students preparing for exams
